threats.title: setLockDuration Allows Bypassing Lock Period via Mismatched Validation and Assignment
threats.hypothesis_description: The setLockDuration function validates the new unlock time using block.timestamp + _duration >= old unlockTime, but computes the actual new unlockTime as lastLockTime + _duration. Once more than half the lock period has elapsed, a user can choose a _duration that passes the validation check but sets unlockTime to a timestamp in the past (relative to block.timestamp), enabling immediate withdrawal.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a direct logic bug where the validation formula and the assignment formula operate on different base timestamps. The validation uses block.timestamp (current time) while the assignment uses lastLockTime (time of original lock). After sufficient time passes, lastLockTime + _duration < block.timestamp even though block.timestamp + _duration >= old unlockTime.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Validation: uint32(block.timestamp) + uint32(_duration) < unlockTime. Assignment: unlockTime = lastLockTime + uint32(_duration). Different base timestamps in check vs assignment.

preconditions: User has locked tokens with quantity > 0
More than half the lock duration has elapsed since lastLockTime
Contract is not paused

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Mismatched base timestamp between validation and state assignment
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Check uses block.timestamp but unlockTime assignment uses lastLockTime; when elapsed > D/2, user can set unlockTime = lastLockTime + small_duration which is in the past
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No check that new unlockTime (lastLockTime + _duration) >= block.timestamp

analysis_questions: What is the exact range of _duration values that pass the check but produce a past unlockTime?
Can this be exploited to claim lockdrop NFT rewards and immediately unlock?
Does the MaxLockDuration constraint limit the exploitability window?
validation_requirements: Demonstrate a concrete scenario: lock for 365 days, wait 300+ days, call setLockDuration to set unlockTime to the past
Verify unlock succeeds immediately after setLockDuration
Confirm NFT rewards from lockdrop are retained after early unlock

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: User locks 1000 ETH during lockdrop for 365 days, receives NFT reveals. At day 300, calls setLockDuration(66 days). Check: (T0+300)+66 = T0+366 >= T0+365 passes. Assignment: unlockTime = T0+66 (234 days in the past). User immediately calls unlock() to withdraw 1000 ETH while keeping NFT rewards.
prerequisites: Elapsed time > D/2 where D is original lock duration

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Users can unlock all locked tokens prematurely, breaking the lockdrop economic model. NFT rewards obtained under false lock commitment.
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Protocol and all participants who locked honestly; diluted NFT/reward value
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None — tokens already withdrawn and NFTs minted

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifies that unlockTime after setLockDuration is always >= block.timestamp

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Single transaction after sufficient elapsed time
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable on-chain by monitoring setLockDuration calls followed by immediate unlock

threats.title: lockOnBehalf Allows Zero-Cost Indefinite Fund Freezing via Unlock Time Reset
threats.hypothesis_description: Any external caller can invoke lockOnBehalf with _quantity=0 for any registered victim, which resets the victim's unlockTime to block.timestamp + lockDuration without adding any tokens. This allows an attacker to indefinitely prevent a victim from ever withdrawing their locked tokens at near-zero cost (only gas).
threats.investigation_rationale: _lock unconditionally overwrites lastLockTime and unlockTime regardless of _quantity. For ETH, msg.value=0 matches _quantity=0. For ERC20, allowance >= 0 always holds. transferFrom with 0 amount succeeds on standard ERC20. The attacker can repeatedly call this to push the victim's unlockTime forward indefinitely.

proof_of_signal.pattern: lockedToken.lastLockTime = uint32(block.timestamp); lockedToken.unlockTime = uint32(block.timestamp) + uint32(_lockDuration); — unconditionally set regardless of _quantity value

preconditions: Victim has tokens locked with quantity > 0
Victim has a non-zero lockDuration in playerSettings (or lockdrop.minLockDuration > 0)
The token contract is active and configured
Contract is not paused

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No minimum _quantity check; unlockTime reset on every _lock call including zero-amount
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: lockOnBehalf(address(0), 0, victim) with msg.value=0 resets victim's unlockTime at zero cost
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No require(_quantity > 0); no check that new unlockTime >= existing unlockTime for existing locks in _lock

analysis_questions: Does transferFrom with 0 amount succeed for all configured token types on Blast?
Can the griefing be automated cheaply (bot calling every N days)?
Is there any recovery mechanism for the victim?
validation_requirements: Call lockOnBehalf(address(0), 0, victim) and verify unlockTime is reset
Confirm victim cannot unlock after the griefing call
Test with ERC20 tokens to confirm 0-amount transferFrom succeeds

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Victim locks 100 ETH with 90-day lock. At day 89, attacker calls lockOnBehalf(address(0), 0, victim) with msg.value=0. Victim's unlockTime resets to now+90 days. Attacker repeats every 89 days indefinitely, permanently preventing victim from withdrawing 100 ETH.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete indefinite freezing of victim's locked funds. Directly violates stated invariant 'Funds must not get locked forever'
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Any user with locked tokens can be targeted
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None unless contract is paused or upgraded; victim cannot withdraw

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for lockOnBehalf with zero quantity or griefing scenarios

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Repeatable every lock period minus 1 block
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible on-chain as repeated lockOnBehalf calls with 0 quantity

threats.title: USD Price Governance Vote Exclusivity Violated: Disapprover Can Also Approve
threats.hypothesis_description: The approveUSDPrice function only checks if the caller has already approved (approvals[msg.sender] == _usdProposalId) but does not check if they have already disapproved. A PriceFeed operator who disapproves can subsequently approve the same proposal, incrementing both approvalsCount and disapprovalsCount. This inflates disapprovalsCount, potentially causing proposals to fail execution in _execUSDPriceUpdate (which requires disapprovalsCount < DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD).
threats.investigation_rationale: The approveUSDPrice function is missing a check for disapprovals[msg.sender] == _usdProposalId. The disapproveUSDPrice function correctly checks for prior approvals, but the reverse is not true. This asymmetric validation creates a voting integrity flaw.

proof_of_signal.pattern: approveUSDPrice checks only approvals[msg.sender] but not disapprovals[msg.sender]; operator can call disapproveUSDPrice then approveUSDPrice

preconditions: Active USD update proposal exists
Caller has a PriceFeed role (1-5)
Caller has not previously approved (but HAS disapproved)

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: approveUSDPrice line 182 checks approvals[msg.sender] but never checks disapprovals[msg.sender]

analysis_questions: Can double-voting cause a proposal with sufficient approvals to fail execution?
Can this create a permanently stuck proposal state?
What is the minimum operator coalition to exploit this?
validation_requirements: Demonstrate operator calling disapprove then approve on same proposal
Verify both counts increment
Test if _execUSDPriceUpdate fails when disapprovalsCount reaches threshold via double-votes

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: With thresholds at 3/3: Proposer auto-approves (1a,0d). Op B disapproves then approves (2a,1d). Op C disapproves then approves (3a,2d). Execution triggered: 3>=3 AND 2<3 passes. But if Op D also disapproves then approves before Op C's approve: (3a,3d). Now Op C's approve makes (4a,3d). _execUSDPriceUpdate: 4>=3 but 3<3 is FALSE. Proposal cannot execute.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Governance deadlock on price updates; stale USD prices affect getLockedWeightedValue calculations and downstream reward distribution
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All protocol participants whose rewards depend on accurate USD pricing
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: New proposal after deletion, but attack can be repeated

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for disapprove-then-approve sequence by same operator

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring vote events for duplicate voters

threats.title: Fee-on-Transfer Token Accounting Insolvency
threats.hypothesis_description: The _lock function records lockedToken.quantity += _quantity (the requested amount) without measuring the actual tokens received via a balance-of-before/after pattern. For fee-on-transfer tokens, the contract receives fewer tokens than recorded. Over time, sum of locked quantities exceeds actual contract balance, causing last unlockers to fail withdrawals.
threats.investigation_rationale: Project documentation explicitly states the contract is expected to handle fee-on-transfer tokens, but the code uses raw _quantity for accounting instead of actual received amount. This is a stated requirement that the code fails to satisfy.
deployment_assumptions: A fee-on-transfer token is configured as an active token
threats.config_evidence: Project docs state: 'expected to handle non-standard ERC20 behaviors, including fee-on-transfer'. Test config (configure-token-test.ts) configures USDB, WETH, ETH — none are fee-on-transfer on Blast currently, but docs indicate future tokens may be.

proof_of_signal.pattern: token.transferFrom(_tokenOwner, address(this), _quantity) followed by lockedToken.quantity += _quantity; actual received amount not measured

preconditions: Admin configures a fee-on-transfer token
Multiple users lock and unlock the token over time

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No balance-before/after measurement for actual received amount
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No SafeERC20, no balance delta check

analysis_questions: Which tokens on Blast have fee-on-transfer behavior?
What is the rate of insolvency accumulation per lock/unlock cycle?
How many lock/unlock cycles before last user cannot withdraw?
validation_requirements: Deploy with a mock fee-on-transfer token
Lock from multiple users, then attempt to unlock all — verify last user reverts
Calculate the exact shortfall per operation

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Admin configures a 2% fee-on-transfer token. User A locks 100 tokens (contract receives 98, records 100). User B locks 100 tokens (contract receives 98, records 100). Contract balance: 196, recorded: 200. When both try to unlock 100 each, second user's transfer fails with insufficient balance.
prerequisites: Multiple users lock the same token

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Last users to unlock permanently lose their tokens — insolvency proportional to total volume × fee rate
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Last N unlockers of the fee-on-transfer token
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires external fund injection to cover shortfall

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test with fee-on-transfer token mock

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Gradual accumulation over multiple lock/unlock cycles
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing sum of locked quantities to actual balance

threats.title: Unlock Does Not Clear Remainder, Enabling Phantom NFT Credit
threats.hypothesis_description: When a user unlocks all their tokens, lockedToken.quantity is decremented to 0 but lockedToken.remainder is not cleared. On the next lock during a lockdrop, this orphaned remainder is added to the new quantity (quantity = _quantity + lockedToken.remainder), potentially awarding NFT reveals based on tokens that are no longer locked.
threats.investigation_rationale: The unlock function only modifies lockedToken.quantity. The remainder from a previous lockdrop persists even after full withdrawal. A user can exploit this by: locking during lockdrop (earning remainder), unlocking everything, then re-locking a small amount during the same or next lockdrop to combine with the phantom remainder.

proof_of_signal.pattern: unlock() modifies only lockedToken.quantity -= _quantity; remainder is never touched. In _lock line 316: quantity = _quantity + lockedToken.remainder

preconditions: User locked tokens during a lockdrop and earned a non-zero remainder
User fully unlocks their tokens (quantity becomes 0)
A lockdrop is active (same or new) when user re-locks

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: unlock() does not clear remainder; _lock() adds stored remainder to new quantity
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: remainder persists after quantity reaches 0, creating phantom credit for NFT minting
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No lockedToken.remainder = 0 in unlock when quantity reaches 0

analysis_questions: What is the maximum economic value extractable via phantom remainder?
Can this be looped (lock→unlock→lock) to amplify the effect?
Does the remainder get consumed (set to 0) after the next lock?
validation_requirements: Lock during lockdrop with amount that creates a remainder
Fully unlock
Lock small amount during lockdrop and verify NFT reveals include phantom remainder
Verify remainder is consumed (set to 0) after second lock

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: User locks 99 tokens during lockdrop (nftCost=100). remainder=99, 0 NFTs. User unlocks all 99 tokens. remainder still 99, quantity 0. User locks 1 token during lockdrop. quantity = 1+99 = 100. numberNFTs = 1. User gets 1 NFT while only having 1 token locked.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Users can mint NFTs with minimal active stake; NFT reward dilution for honest lockers
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: NFTs already minted cannot be revoked

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for unlock-then-relock with carried remainder

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Minimal — only 1 token needed for re-lock
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by checking if remainder > 0 when quantity = 0

threats.title: Locking Outside Active Lockdrop Period Zeroes Stored Remainder
threats.hypothesis_description: In _lock, the local variable remainder is initialized to 0. The block that computes the new remainder only executes during an active lockdrop. Outside the lockdrop period, remainder stays 0. At the end of _lock, lockedToken.remainder is unconditionally overwritten with this local variable, destroying any previously accumulated remainder from a lockdrop.
threats.investigation_rationale: The unconditional assignment lockedToken.remainder = remainder at line 343 overwrites the stored value. When the lockdrop block (lines 324-335) is skipped, remainder = 0, destroying any prior value. This is a state corruption bug.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint256 remainder; (defaults to 0) at line 315; conditional NFT calculation at lines 324-335; unconditional lockedToken.remainder = remainder at line 343

preconditions: User accumulated a non-zero remainder from a previous lockdrop lock
User locks additional tokens outside an active lockdrop period

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Local variable defaults to 0; conditional assignment only during lockdrop; unconditional storage write
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: lockedToken.remainder silently zeroed for any lock outside lockdrop
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should preserve existing remainder when lockdrop not active, or scope the assignment inside the lockdrop conditional

analysis_questions: What is the typical remainder value in economic terms?
Can users avoid this by not locking outside lockdrop?
Does this interact with the phantom remainder bug (TECH-H005)?
validation_requirements: Lock during lockdrop to create remainder
Lock additional tokens after lockdrop ends
Verify remainder is now 0

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: User locks 150 tokens during lockdrop (nftCost=100). Gets 1 NFT, remainder=50. Lockdrop ends. User locks 60 more tokens. Outside lockdrop, no NFT calc runs. lockedToken.remainder = 0 (local default). User loses 50 tokens of NFT progress permanently.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of partial NFT credit (remainder value); up to nftCost-1 tokens worth per user per token
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Any user who locks outside lockdrop with existing remainder
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Permanent loss — remainder cannot be recovered

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for locking outside lockdrop period with pre-existing remainder

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing remainder before/after lock outside lockdrop

threats.title: lockOnBehalf Can Overwrite Recipient's Lock Duration Preference
threats.hypothesis_description: In _lock, if the lock recipient's playerSettings.lockDuration is 0 (never set by the user), _lockDuration falls back to lockdrop.minLockDuration. At the end of _lock, playerSettings[_lockRecipient].lockDuration is unconditionally set to this value. An attacker can use lockOnBehalf to force-set a victim's lock duration to the lockdrop minimum before the victim sets their own preference.
threats.investigation_rationale: The combination of the fallback logic and the unconditional write to playerSettings means any caller of lockOnBehalf can influence the recipient's future lock behavior. This is a sovereignty violation where the recipient's preferences are overwritten without consent.
preconditions: Recipient has never called setLockDuration (lockDuration == 0)
Recipient is a registered main account
Token is active and configured

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Unconditional write to recipient's playerSettings from lockOnBehalf
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Attacker sets victim's lockDuration to lockdrop.minLockDuration before victim configures their own preference
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should only write playerSettings for msg.sender, not for _lockRecipient in lockOnBehalf

analysis_questions: Can the victim override this by calling setLockDuration afterward?
Does this interact with the unlockTime griefing (TECH-H002)?
What is the practical impact on victim's future locks?
validation_requirements: Call lockOnBehalf for a new user who hasn't set lockDuration
Verify victim's playerSettings.lockDuration is now set to lockdrop.minLockDuration
Verify victim must explicitly call setLockDuration to change it

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: New user registers. Attacker calls lockOnBehalf(token, 0, victim) before victim sets lockDuration. Victim's lockDuration is set to lockdrop.minLockDuration (e.g., 30 days). Victim later locks large amount expecting to use a longer duration but doesn't realize their duration was already set.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Victim may lock with unintended short duration, affecting reward calculations
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: New users who haven't yet configured lock duration
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Victim can call setLockDuration to fix

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for lockOnBehalf's effect on recipient's playerSettings

threats.title: Missing SafeERC20: Non-Standard Return Value Tokens Cause Revert
threats.hypothesis_description: The contract uses raw IERC20.transfer() and IERC20.transferFrom() without SafeERC20 wrappers. Tokens that do not return a boolean value (like USDT on Ethereum) will cause ABI decoding failures, making lock and unlock operations revert for those tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: Project documentation states the contract should handle 'missing return values'. Solidity 0.8.25 with the IERC20 interface expects a bool return from transfer/transferFrom. Non-standard tokens that return nothing (0 bytes) will cause a revert during ABI decoding.
deployment_assumptions: A non-standard-return ERC20 token (like USDT) is configured
threats.config_evidence: Project docs: 'expected to handle non-standard ERC20 behaviors, including... missing return values'. Current test config uses USDB/WETH which are standard, but docs indicate future tokens should be supported.

proof_of_signal.pattern: token.transferFrom(_tokenOwner, address(this), _quantity) and token.transfer(msg.sender, _quantity) without SafeERC20 wrapper

preconditions: Admin configures a token that does not return bool from transfer/transferFrom
User attempts to lock or unlock that token

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: ABI decoding expects bool return; non-standard tokens return 0 bytes → revert

analysis_questions: Which tokens on Blast have non-standard return values?
If a non-standard token is already locked, can users unlock?
Does this cause permanent fund freezing for already-deposited tokens?
validation_requirements: Deploy with a mock non-standard-return token
Attempt lock — verify revert
If tokens were deposited via alternative means, verify unlock also reverts

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Admin configures USDT-like token. Users attempt to lock: transferFrom reverts due to missing return value. If any tokens reached the contract via other means (migration), unlock also reverts, freezing funds permanently.
prerequisites: Non-standard return ERC20 token configured and active

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Token becomes unusable for locking; potential permanent freeze if tokens already deposited
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users of the non-standard token
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Admin can deactivate token; users may need migration

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test with non-standard return ERC20 token

threats.title: Zero Lock Duration When Lockdrop Not Configured Allows Instant Lock/Unlock
threats.hypothesis_description: If no lockdrop has been configured (lockdrop.minLockDuration == 0) and a user has never called setLockDuration (playerSettings.lockDuration == 0), the _lock function sets _lockDuration to 0. This results in unlockTime = block.timestamp + 0 = block.timestamp. The user can unlock in the same or next block, effectively locking with zero duration while triggering forceHarvest for schnibbles.
threats.investigation_rationale: The duration fallback chain is: playerSettings.lockDuration → lockdrop.minLockDuration. If both are 0, _lockDuration = 0. There is no minimum duration enforcement outside the lockdrop period. The forceHarvest call in both lock and unlock may award schnibbles based on the locked weighted value, even if the lock duration is effectively zero.
deployment_assumptions: Lockdrop not yet configured or lockdrop.minLockDuration is 0
threats.config_evidence: start-lockdrop.ts shows lockdrop configuration is a manual post-deploy step. Between contract deployment and lockdrop configuration, lockdrop.minLockDuration defaults to 0.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (_lockDuration == 0) { _lockDuration = lockdrop.minLockDuration; } — if both are 0, _lockDuration stays 0; no minimum enforcement outside lockdrop

preconditions: lockdrop.minLockDuration == 0 (no lockdrop configured or minLockDuration set to 0)
User has not called setLockDuration
Token is active and configured

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No minimum duration enforcement; 0 duration results in immediate unlockability
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: unlockTime = block.timestamp when _lockDuration = 0; unlock check (unlockTime > block.timestamp) fails immediately
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No require(_lockDuration > 0) or minimum duration constant

analysis_questions: Is there a window between contract deployment and lockdrop configuration?
Does forceHarvest award meaningful schnibbles for zero-duration locks?
Can this be exploited repeatedly for schnibbles farming?
validation_requirements: Deploy without configuring lockdrop
Lock and immediately unlock in consecutive transactions
Verify schnibbles awarded via forceHarvest

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Before lockdrop is configured, user locks 100 ETH (lockDuration=0, unlockTime=now). Next block, user calls unlock. Both lock and unlock trigger forceHarvest, potentially awarding schnibbles based on 100 ETH weighted value. Repeat.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Schnibbles farming with zero lock commitment; inflated reward distribution
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All honest lockers receive diluted rewards

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for lock behavior before lockdrop configuration

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Reusable — same tokens locked and unlocked repeatedly
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring rapid lock/unlock patterns

threats.title: USD Price Proposal Applies Single Price to All Listed Tokens, Enabling Price Corruption
threats.hypothesis_description: proposeUSDPrice accepts a single _price and an array of _contracts. _execUSDPriceUpdate applies this single price to ALL listed contracts. Approvers validate only the price value (not which contracts are in the proposal). A malicious PriceFeed proposer can include unrelated tokens in the contracts array, and honest approvers who verify the price matches one token will unknowingly approve the wrong price for others.
threats.investigation_rationale: The approveUSDPrice function checks usdUpdateProposal.proposedPrice != _price but has no mechanism for approvers to verify or acknowledge which contracts are included. The proposal's contracts array is opaque to approvers unless they manually query the struct.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Single proposedPrice applied to all contracts in array; approvers only validate price, not contracts list

preconditions: Malicious PriceFeed operator proposes price with multiple contracts
Honest approvers check price matches expected token without verifying full contracts array
APPROVE_THRESHOLD approvals reached

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Single price for multiple contracts; blind approval by approvers
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Proposer includes [WETH, USDB] with WETH's price ($3000). Approvers verify $3000 for WETH, approve. USDB gets set to $3000 (3000x overvaluation).
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No per-contract price mapping; no contracts array hash in approval

analysis_questions: Do PriceFeed operators use automated scripts that only check price, not contracts?
What downstream impact does incorrect usdPrice have on getLockedWeightedValue?
Can this inflate a user's weighted value for reward manipulation?
validation_requirements: Propose price for [WETH, USDB] with WETH's price
Have approvers approve the WETH price
Verify USDB's usdPrice is set to WETH's price
Calculate getLockedWeightedValue inflation

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Malicious PriceFeed operator proposes $3000 for [WETH_address, USDB_address]. Other operators see $3000, verify it's correct for WETH, approve. Execution sets USDB usdPrice to $3000 instead of $1. USDB lockers' getLockedWeightedValue inflated by 3000x, distorting reward distribution.
prerequisites: At least one malicious PriceFeed operator

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Massive reward inflation/deflation; users with overpriced tokens receive disproportionate rewards
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: New proposal to correct prices, but rewards already distributed are unrecoverable

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for multi-token price proposal with mismatched prices

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring USDPriceUpdated events for unexpected token/price combinations

threats.title: setUSDThresholds Allows Zero or Extreme Values Breaking Governance
threats.hypothesis_description: setUSDThresholds has no bounds validation on _approve and _disapprove parameters. Setting DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD to 0 makes the condition disapprovalsCount < 0 impossible for uint8 (always false), preventing ANY proposal from executing. Setting APPROVE_THRESHOLD to 0 with high DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD allows proposals to execute with just the proposer's auto-approval since approvalsCount starts at 1.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _execUSDPriceUpdate condition requires both approvalsCount >= APPROVE_THRESHOLD AND disapprovalsCount < DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD. With DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD=0, the second condition is always false (uint8 disapprovalsCount >= 0 is always true), permanently blocking all price updates.

proof_of_signal.pattern: No bounds check on threshold values; _execUSDPriceUpdate: disapprovalsCount < DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD where DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD could be 0

preconditions: Admin calls setUSDThresholds with extreme values (0 or 255)
No proposal currently in progress (required by the function)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No minimum/maximum bounds on threshold values
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD=0 makes disapprovalsCount < 0 always false for uint8; all proposals permanently blocked

analysis_questions: Is admin a multisig or EOA?
Can admin fix by calling setUSDThresholds again?
Is there a timelock on admin actions?
validation_requirements: Set DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD to 0
Propose and attempt to approve a price update
Verify _execUSDPriceUpdate never executes

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Compromised or malicious admin sets DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD=0. Now _execUSDPriceUpdate requires disapprovalsCount < 0 which is impossible for uint8. All price updates are permanently blocked. USD prices become stale, distorting getLockedWeightedValue and downstream rewards.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent inability to update USD prices; stale prices distort reward distribution
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Admin can call setUSDThresholds again to fix, but requires recognizing the issue

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for extreme threshold values

threats.title: proposeUSDPrice Does Not Call _execUSDPriceUpdate When APPROVE_THRESHOLD Equals 1
threats.hypothesis_description: The proposeUSDPrice function auto-approves by setting approvalsCount=1 and the proposer's approval flag. However, it never calls _execUSDPriceUpdate. This function is only called inside approveUSDPrice. If APPROVE_THRESHOLD is set to 1, the proposer's auto-approval should be sufficient to execute, but no execution occurs. A second operator must call approveUSDPrice to trigger execution, effectively requiring minimum 2 approvals regardless of threshold.
threats.investigation_rationale: The semantic expectation of APPROVE_THRESHOLD=1 is that a single proposer can set the price. But the code requires at least one additional approveUSDPrice call. This is a functional correctness bug where the threshold semantics don't match implementation.
threats.config_evidence: setUSDThresholds allows setting to 1; no bounds check prevents it

proof_of_signal.pattern: proposeUSDPrice sets approvalsCount++ but never checks if threshold is met or calls _execUSDPriceUpdate

preconditions: APPROVE_THRESHOLD set to 1 by admin
PriceFeed operator proposes a price

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Missing threshold check and _execUSDPriceUpdate call in proposeUSDPrice
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: approvalsCount=1 meets threshold=1 but execution never triggered
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should call _execUSDPriceUpdate at end of proposeUSDPrice if threshold met

analysis_questions: Is APPROVE_THRESHOLD=1 a realistic configuration?
What happens if the proposer tries to approve their own proposal?
Is there a workaround?
validation_requirements: Set APPROVE_THRESHOLD to 1
Call proposeUSDPrice
Verify proposal is NOT executed despite approvalsCount >= threshold
Verify proposer cannot call approveUSDPrice (ProposerCannotApproveError)

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Admin sets APPROVE_THRESHOLD=1. PriceFeed operator proposes price. approvalsCount=1>=1 but _execUSDPriceUpdate not called. Proposer cannot approve (ProposerCannotApproveError). Another operator must approve, requiring 2 approvals despite threshold=1. Price updates become slower than intended.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Price updates delayed; stale prices affect reward calculations
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users dependent on accurate USD pricing
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Another operator can approve; operational workaround exists

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible when proposal sits without execution
